know I've met with, uh, Ada and Joe, you guys are fairly new, I think what, three or four weeks in onboarding with us at this point.

eyes in cleaning up a lot of the kind of underbelly to get domain rankings, site health, all those technical improvements so that, you know, we're getting the most out of the content that we're revamping.

Our demand gen team, which is a large majority of our total marketing team, which sits, I think, around like, I don't know, 110 seats internally, is getting reorganized from a functional standpoint to more of a business unit functional standpoint.

We're focused on specific functions across all products, all revenue lines, and every business unit.

Do we see any conflict or issues?

So that everybody has clarity and visibility around who's doing what.
We're not kind of stepping on toes because this is a, you know, a larger organization.

pipeline in a strategic manner.

did, Galvanize was the recent one on the risk side.

then we can also use the paid search side to run very quick tests and get anecdotal answers or empirical answers to these kind of hypotheses that we want to test and market with SEO.

An SEO team should identify, you know, pages that are similar and you should always redirect to a similar piece of content.

But also to your point of the blog post, you still want to continually blog in the space, even if it's, you know, thought leadership or, you know, direct content to your ICP and your category or just content in general about your space, only because that shows more authority and that you're an active participant in the topic.

When you stop doing those things and really concentrate on product pages, it kind of bleeds over to where, you know, you're not as active, you're not getting as many links, and you're not as, you know, authoritative.
So that kind of backfires a little bit.
So continuing to invest in blog content is still best practice.
